records.Name: RAQ-2610657
records.Action__c: Cold water systems should be maintained, where possible, at a temperature below 20oC. If the incoming water temperature is above 20oC, employ a competent person to verify the cause of the high temperature or inform the Local Water Authority to ensure the problem is rectified.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, temperature profiles of incoming mains water assessor to describe achieved a temperature of XXoC thus posing a risk of legionella proliferation.
records.Question__c: Are post flush temperatures of mains cold water outlets satisfactory for the control of legionella bacteria at less than 20oC within two minutes of flushing?
records.Action2__c: All drinking water outlets i.e. water fountains or drinking water taps should be removed and pipework should be cut off as close as possible to the main supply to prevent dead-legs.
records.Observation2__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, water fountains   /   drinking water taps were found in ASSESSOR TO STATE. Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulations 1992 states that drinking water taps should not be installed in places where contamination is likely, for example in a workshop where lead is handled or processed. As far as is reasonably practicable, they should also not be within Toilets or Workshops.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are post flush temperatures of mains cold water outlets satisfactory for the control of legionella bacteria at less than 20oC within two minutes of flushing?

records.Name: RAQ-2610659
records.Action__c: Access to the cistern should be improved to allow regular inspections, monitoring and cleaning & disinfection purposes.

records.Name: RAQ-2610660
records.Action__c: Ensure cold water storage cisterns are easily accessible to allow safe access for cleaning and disinfection and general inspection purposes.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the risk assessment, access to the cold water storage cistern CWS-01 was inadequate.
records.Question__c: Are cisterns adequately supported and installed in a position to allow inspection or maintenance?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are cisterns adequately supported and installed in a position to allow inspection or maintenance?

records.Name: RAQ-2610661
records.Action__c: Cold water storage cisterns should be re-configured such that the cisterns are linked in parallel, or by replacing the two cisterns with an appropriately sized single cistern.
records.Observation__c: The cold water storage cistern(s) CWS-01 & CWS-02 are linked in series which is considered poor practice. Stagnation of the cold water storage cistern CWS-02 is as a result of the configuration of the cistern, with cisterns 1 & 2 being linked in series. Cold water is supplied to cistern CWS-02 from cistern CWS-01 at low level. The outlet pipework from cistern CWS-02 is located at low level, thus turnover of water is restricted to low level movement, as cold water is more dense that warm water, stratification occurs, thus the upper layer of water is allowed to stagnate.
records.Question__c: Are cisterns linked in series avoided?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are cisterns linked in series avoided?

records.Name: RAQ-2610662
records.Action__c: Undertake an investigation of the usage of cold water services within the building to ascertain the overall usage of water from the cold water storage cistern. Should the turn-over of stored water within the cold water storage cistern be deemed to be too low, the capacity could be reduced by replacing the ball float arm with an extended or angled ball float. Alternatively, the cistern could be replaced with an appropriately sized, sectional glass reinforced plastic cistern.
records.Observation__c: The capacity of the cold water storage cistern(s) CWS-01 located in assessor to describe is deemed excessive for the building’s requirements. Cold water cisterns should show complete water turnover within 24 hours, otherwise stagnation can occur.
records.Question__c: Are the cistern(s) appropriately sized allowing stored water to turn over every 24 hours?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are the cistern(s) appropriately sized allowing stored water to turn over every 24 hours?

records.Name: RAQ-2610663

records.Name: RAQ-2610665
records.Action__c: Replace the hollow structural pipe/support with a suitable, solid structural support with all materials being WRAS approved.
records.Observation__c: A hollow tube structural support is fitted to the sectional glass reinforced plastic cold water storage cistern (CWS-01) located within the assessor to describe. These supports are fitted to prevent the lid/roof of the cistern from sagging, however, they can fill with water which over time becomes stagnant then leaches into the stored water – thus causing water contamination. This contamination can be worse during high demand periods as the level of water drops which allows the stagnant water within the hollow tube to contaminate system further.
records.Question__c: Are cisterns fitted with tight fitting lids?
records.Action2__c: The cistern should be fitted with a tight fitting lid to prevent aerial contamination. It should be ensured that all materials comply with the WRc / WRAS Approved Materials Directory.
records.Observation2__c: Cold water storage cistern 1 (CWS-01) located in assessor to describe does not comply with paragraph 16 schedule 2 of The Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are cisterns fitted with tight fitting lids?

records.Name: RAQ-2610670
records.Action__c: The strainers or filters associated with the TMVs should be inspected, clean, descaled and disinfected annually. This includes a visual inspection to identify reduced flow of the water or operation of the TMV and a fail safe of the TMV. Where the cold water feed to the TMV is isolated, the TMV must prevent discharge of hot water.
records.Observation__c: There are no records to indicate that the TMVs are subject to regular inspection and maintenance.
records.Question__c: Are the Thermostatic Mixing Valves (TMVs) subject to regular inspection and maintenance?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are the Thermostatic Mixing Valves (TMVs) subject to regular inspection and maintenance?

records.Name: RAQ-2610671
records.Action__c: Remove the TMV described, ensuring that no blind ends or dead legs remain and that temperatures do not exceed the recommended limit of 60oC. If temperatures do exceed 60oC, consideration must be taken to fit “Caution Very Hot Water” labels to the appropriate outlets. Alternatively, where a risk of scalding is still deemed high, consideration should be given where practicable to incorporate TMVs directly in the tap fitting, as mixing at the point of the outlet is preferable.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, TMV-01 within the assessor to describe was no longer required. The risk of scalding was deemed to be insignificant as temperatures to the TMV were found to be at less than 60oC. Potential increase of legionella proliferation can be found within the blended water downstream of the TMV, particularly where a singular TMV is serving multiple outlets.
records.Question__c: Has consideration been given to removing TMV’s in areas where the consequence of scalding is less than the potential risk of legionella infection?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Has consideration been given to removing TMV’s in areas where the consequence of scalding is less than the potential risk of legionella infection?

records.Name: RAQ-2610677
records.Action__c: Flush through and purge to drain on a monthly basis / 3 monthly basis / six monthly basis with all such actions recorded in the Water Logbook.
records.Observation__c: Expansion/accumulator vessel(s) were observed installed within the domestic water system and was of the flow through type.
records.Question__c: Are there any deadlegs identified to supply pipework within the water system?
records.Action2__c: A competent & approved contractor or person should be employed to install a bleed valve or drain valve to the top of the swan neck / pig-tail pipe-work which allows them to be flushed on a weekly/twice weekly basis.
records.Action3__c: The supply pipework should be valved off, cut back at the point of connection with existing services and capped off such that no deadlegs remain.
records.Action4__c: The outlets should be flushed for a minimum of 2 minutes on a weekly basis to prevent the stagnation of water within the supply pipework. Alternatively, the supply pipework to the little used outlet should be valved off, cut back at the point of connection with existing services and capped off such that no deadlegs remain.
records.Action5__c: Flush through and purge to drain on a monthly basis / 3 monthly basis / six monthly basis with all such actions recorded in the Water Logbook.
records.Action6__c: Employ a competent & approved contractor or person to install a bleed or drain valve to the supply pipework and re-orientate the expansion vessel, such that the connecting pipework to the expansion vessel rises, thus enabling effective draining/blow down.
records.Action7__c: Remove the little used outlet, making good any dead pipework that may remain. Alternatively, the cold outlet should be subject to a weekly flushing regime and document
records.Action8__c: Employ a competent approved contractor or person to install a bleed or drain valve to the supply pipework and re-orientate the expansion vessel, such that the connecting pipework to the expansion vessel rises, thus enabling effective draining/blow down.
records.Observation2__c: ONLY TO BE USED ON HEALTHCARE TYPE PREMISES - Swan necks and/or pig-tails have been observed associated with the installed water plant – e.g. HW Calorifier / HWS Return / HWS(R) circuit / Boosted Cold Water Booster Pump Set. Swan-necks and pigtails are not considered to be of a significant source of legionellae if the system water temperature is always maintained within acceptable parameters outlined within ACoP L8 or other appropriate documentation (e.g. less than 20oC within cold water and between 50-60oC in hot water systems).  Problems can arise when this temperature is not achieved and it falls outside the documented parameters, thus being within a temperature range that Legionella bacteria may proliferate. Hot water temperatures were found to be lower than 50oC within the calorifier / HWS (R) circuit pipework thus increasing the risk of Legionella proliferation & accumulations of biofilms. Adjust the thermostat of hot water Calorifier such that hot water achieves a temperature of 50 - 60oC to reduce this risk The cold water system is currently above 20oC and the plant room was also found to be over 20oC therefore the swan-necks and/or pigtails installed on the cold system present an increased risk of Legionella growth.
records.Observation3__c: The following pipework has been identified as a deadleg: ASSESSOR TO DESCRIBE
records.Observation4__c: The following hot & cold water outlets within the Disabled WC   /   assessor to describe are deemed low usage and there are no formal records to indicate that they are subject to a flushing regime: assessor to describe
records.Observation5__c: An expansion vessel associated with assessor to describe was of the diaphragm or bladder type and equal to or greater than 25l in capacity.
records.Observation6__c: No drain valve was observed to the supply pipework to the expansion/accumulator vessel associated with domestic boosted / cold / hot water system / boiler system pressurisation unit / chilled water pressurisation unit / other
records.Observation7__c: A TMV (Thermostatic Mixing Valve) is fitted to a wash hand basin in the  assessor to describe which also has a cold outlet or a separate cold feed, this cold outlet is likely to have low usage due to only hand washing taking place at this outlet and has effectively become a deadleg.
records.Observation8__c: An expansion vessel associated with xxxxxx has been installed up-side down, increasing the likelihood of stagnation and the formation of biofilms and/or the accumulation of particulates.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are there any deadlegs identified to supply pipework within the water system?

records.Name: RAQ-2610678
records.Action__c: Either have the hoses replaced with ones that are compliant with BS6920 parts 1 to 3  or have them replaced with fixed copper pipework. Please Note: WRAS Approved flexi-hoses does not guarantee that flexi-hoses are EPDM free. Consideration should be given to sourcing flexi-hoses with polyethylene (PE), cross-linked polyethylene (PEX), linear low density polyethylene (LLDPE) or post-chlorinated PVC (PVC C).
records.Observation__c: Flexible hoses were observed at the following locations within the property: specify We were unable to verify if they were compliant with BS6920 parts 1 to 3 and they may contain the material EPDM which encourages growth of Legionella spp.
records.Question__c: Are there any unsuitable flexible hoses identified in areas which may proliferate bacterial/micro organism growth?
records.Action2__c: Unless there is a RPZ or other suitable non-return valve installed prior to the flexi-hose connection, disconnect the flexi-hose filler loop when it is not in use. This will reduce the risk of stagnation of water within the flexi-hoses to prevent back-siphonage in the event of mains cold water de-pressurisation or back siphonage.
records.Observation2__c: The filling loop flexi-hose to the boiler system pressurisation unit remains connected, potentially containing highly stagnant water.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are there any unsuitable flexible hoses identified in areas which may proliferate bacterial/micro organism growth?

records.Name: RAQ-2610680
records.Action__c: Implement an effective monitoring regime or scheme of works where bacterial growth can be controlled. Mothballing procedures are a compromise between the adequate control of microbial growth and the use of water for flushing (while avoiding wastage that can occur when weekly flushing for long periods) and degradation of the system by any disinfectant added. Where disinfectants are in use, these should leave the system fit for its intended purpose. It is recommended that the system remains filled during mothballing. Draining down the system can leave moisture within the system, thus potentially allowing biofilm to develop where there is an increase in temperature or pockets of water left behind. This process also helps avoid problems associated with drying out the system i.e corrosion in metal pipework and the failure of cistern joints. This also can lead to the drying out of foul water and waste water traps leading to unpleasant odours. Systems should be recommissioned as though new and should be thoroughly flushed, cleaned and disinfected before being returned to use.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, the building or part of the building was no longer in regular use, sometimes referred to as “mothballing”. There appears to be no management structure implemented so that microbial growth, including legionella within the water system, is controlled.
records.Question__c: Is the building in full occupancy?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is the building in full occupancy?

records.Name: RAQ-2610693
records.Action__c: The cistern(s) should be subjected to a full cleaning and disinfection in accordance with “The Prevention and Control of Legionellosis”, Approved Code of Practice (L8).
records.Observation__c: Cold water storage cistern CWS-01 is deemed to be in a poor hygienic condition with a moderate   /   high level of sediment observed in the base of the cistern, and low   /   moderate   /   high levels of stagnation of the water surface and a bio-film with heavy discolouration at the air / water interface.
records.Question__c: Are the cisterns in a satisfactory, hygienic condition?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are the cisterns in a satisfactory, hygienic condition?

records.Name: RAQ-2610694
records.Action__c: Insulate supply pipework to ensure temperatures are maintained within the correct range of less than 20oC
records.Observation__c: Cold water temperatures within risk assessor to describe were found to be above 20oC within two minutes of flushing.
records.Question__c: Are post flush temperatures of cold water services outlets satisfactory for the control of legionella bacteria at less than 20oC within two minutes of flushing?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are post flush temperatures of cold water services outlets satisfactory for the control of legionella bacteria at less than 20oC within two minutes of flushing?

records.Name: RAQ-2610695
records.Action__c: Verify that the hot water services calorifier is subject to periodic blow down/drain down to remove sediment & debris from the base of the calorifier. Where blow down shows a need or where temperature profiles achieve temperatures of less than 50 degrees C on a regular basis, ensure the calorifier is subject to chlorination or thermal pasteurisation. All such actions must be recorded into an appropriate domestic water services logbook.
records.Observation__c: There are no records of regular draining down or blow down of the hot water services calorifier CAL-01 to remove sediment or debris from the base of the cylinder.
records.Question__c: Are calorifiers regularly blown down to remove sediment?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are calorifiers regularly blown down to remove sediment?

records.Name: RAQ-2610696
records.Action__c: Ensure the hot water calorifIers are connected in parallel and deliver water at a temperature of at least 60oC
records.Observation__c: Hot water services calorifIers located within the plant room were found to be connected in series and this is deemed poor practice.
records.Question__c: Where more than one calorifier is installed are they connected in parallel?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Where more than one calorifier is installed are they connected in parallel?

records.Name: RAQ-2610697
records.Action__c: Consideration should be given to fitting temperature gauges to the HWS Calorifier to indicate the temperature of stored water and hot water flow to ensure ACoP L8 compliance. Where this is not cost effective, a contact probe must be used to ensure that hot water is stored and supplied within parameters outlined in the ACoP L8 & the supporting guidance document HSG274 Part 2. Records of hot water service flow, return and stored water temperatures should be recorded within a suitable water safety logbook
records.Observation__c: It could not be determined if hot water was stored within the calorifier(s) CAL-01 are equal to or greater than 60oC as no temperature gauges are fitted to the hot water services cylinder.
records.Question__c: Is hot water stored within the calorifier at equal to or greater than 60°C and supplied to the outlets at between 50°C and 60°C?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is hot water stored within the calorifier at equal to or greater than 60°C and supplied to the outlets at between 50°C and 60°C?

records.Name: RAQ-2610698
records.Action__c: Adjust the thermostat associated with CAL-01, such that temperatures are stored and maintained at 60oC or greater, as stated in HSG 274 part 2.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, stored hot water temperatures within the calorifier(s) CAL-01, were found to be below the recommended limit of 60oC, at risk assessor to describe, thus causing the risk of potential legionella proliferation.
records.Question__c: Is hot water stored within the calorifier at equal to or greater than 60oC?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is hot water stored within the calorifier at equal to or greater than 60oC?

records.Name: RAQ-2610699
records.Action__c: Consideration should be given to fitting temperature gauges to the hot water service cylinders / calorifier flow and return pipework to confirm that the HWS return. temperature is at least 50°C and that the anti-stratification measures have been effective and to ensure ACoP L8 compliance. Where this is not cost effective, a contact probe must be used to ensure that hot water is stored 60°C and supplied to the outlets at 50-60°C.
records.Observation__c: There are no records to indicate that hot water flow and return temperatures are recorded on a regular basis as outlined in ACoP L8.
records.Question__c: Is the return temperature to the calorifier maintained at a minimum of 50oC?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is the return temperature to the calorifier maintained at a minimum of 50oC?

records.Name: RAQ-2610700
records.Action__c: Consideration should be given to fitting an anti-stratification / shunt pump to the hot water services calorifier(s) complete with a non-return valve and automatic timer to operate for 1 hour per day, during periods of low demand (L8 page 40; paragraph 158 – 160), thus circulating hot water to the base of the calorifier to promote adequate circulation of stored water and prevent stratification.
records.Observation__c: There is no anti-stratification circuit and shunt pump fitted to hot water services calorifier(s) CAL-01 & CAL-02 and due to it's size and pipework configuration, one is required to prevent stratification within the calorifier.
records.Question__c: Is an anti-stratification circuit with shunt pump fitted to the calorifier(s) to ensure that the temperature at the bottom of the calorifier is raised to 60oC for at least one hour per day?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is an anti-stratification circuit with shunt pump fitted to the calorifier(s) to ensure that the temperature at the bottom of the calorifier is raised to 60oC for at least one hour per day?

records.Name: RAQ-2610701
records.Action__c: Where there are standby recirculating pumps on the hot water circuits, they should be run up once per week.
records.Observation__c: There are no records to show hot water services calorifier CAL-01 standby recirculating pumps are used weekly.
records.Question__c: Are standby water circulation pumps changed over at least weekly to avoid stagnation?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are standby water circulation pumps changed over at least weekly to avoid stagnation?

records.Name: RAQ-2610702
records.Action__c: Undertake an investigation of the usage of hot water services to ascertain if the capacity of the hot water storage calorifiers is sufficient to meet the overall usage of water within the building. Alternatively, the hot water calorifier should be replaced with an appropriately sized cylinder, or consider installing independent point of use water heaters.
records.Observation__c: Capacity was found to be in-adequate from hot water services calorifier CAL-01 to supply hot water to all outlets throughout the building.
records.Question__c: Is the capacity of the calorifiers adequate to prevent temperature fluctuations during normal operating conditions?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is the capacity of the calorifiers adequate to prevent temperature fluctuations during normal operating conditions?

records.Name: RAQ-2610703
records.Action__c: The thermostat associated with hot water calorifier CAL-01   /   IHWS-01   /   POU-01 should be adjusted such that hot water is stored at a temperature of 60oC and supplied at the outlets at a temperature of 50oC to 60oC.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, hot water throughout the building was achieving temperatures of approximately 34-37oC, therefore posing an elevated probability of legionella bacteria proliferation.
records.Question__c: Is the water temperature at the hot water outlets maintained at 50oC within one minute?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is the water temperature at the hot water outlets maintained at 50oC within one minute?

records.Name: RAQ-2610704
records.Action__c: The thermostat associated with the hot water calorifier should be adjusted such that hot water is stored at a temperature of 60oC and supplied at the outlets at a temperature of between 50oC and 60oC. Should hot water be required to be supplied to outlets above 60oC, it should be ensured that all outlets are clearly labelled as “Caution – Very Hot Water” in accordance with The Health & Safety (Safety Signs and Signals) Regulations; 1996.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment survey, temperature profiles of hot water outlets in assessor to describe supplied from hot water services calorifier CAL-01 achieved temperatures greater than 60oC, thus posing a significant scalding risk.
records.Question__c: Where applicable, are hot water outlets that pose a significant scalding risk appropriately labelled as “Caution – Hot Water”?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Where applicable, are hot water outlets that pose a significant scalding risk appropriately labelled as “Caution – Hot Water”?

records.Name: RAQ-2610705
records.Action__c: The outlet should be clearly labelled as 'Caution - Very Hot Water" in accordance with The Health and Safety (Safety Signs and Signals) Regulations, 1996.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, temperature profiles of independent hot water heaters / point of use heaters (IHWS-01) in the assessor to describe achieved a temperature of xx0C thus posing a significant scalding risk (temperatures exceeding 60oC). Please Note: there is an adjustable thermostat to the water heater, allowing the user to adjust the dial to determine how hot or cold the water is supplied. On the maximum temperature setting, hot water is supplied at a temperature greater than 60oC, thus posing a scalding risk to users.
records.Question__c: Is hot water supplied from localised independent hot water heaters / point of use heaters, supplied at a satisfactory temperature to control legionella bacteria?
records.Action2__c: The thermostat associated with the water Heater should be adjusted such that hot water is stored at a temperature of 60oC and supplied at the outlets at a temperature of between 50oC and 60oC. Alternatively, the outlet should be clearly labelled as “Caution – Very Hot Water” in accordance with The Health & Safety (Safety Signs and Signals) Regulations;
records.Action3__c: Repair or replace the faulty IWHS-01
records.Action4__c: The thermostat associated with the water heater should be adjusted such that hot water is stored at a temperature of 60oC and supplied at the outlets at a temperature of 50oC to 60oC.
records.Action5__c: The thermostat associated with the instantaneous water heater should be able to achieve a minimum temperature of 50oC as outlined in the Approved Code of Practice ACoP L8 and support guidance document HSG274. Where the water heater cannot consistently achieve a minimum temperature of 50oC, repair or replace the water heater.
records.Observation2__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, temperature profiles of independent hot water heaters / point of use heaters (IHWS-01) in the assessor to describe achieved a temperature of xx0C thus posing a significant scalding risk (temperatures exceeding 60oC).
records.Observation3__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, the Independent hot water heater 1 (IHWS-01) that supplies hot water to the first floor gents toilet was found to be leaking when operated.
records.Observation4__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, temperature profiles of point of use heater 1 Accessible Toilet achieved a temperature of 39.9 oC thus posing a risk of legionella proliferation (temperatures less than 50oC).
records.Observation5__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, hot water from an instantaneous water heater within the assessor to describe achieved temperatures of approximately xxoC and was not capable of achieving 50oC/or did not appear to be capable of achieving 50oC thus poses a risk of legionella proliferation.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is hot water supplied from localised independent hot water heaters / point of use heaters, supplied at a satisfactory temperature to control legionella bacteria?

records.Name: RAQ-2610706
records.Action__c: Consideration should be given to fitting a drain valve to each of the calorifiers, thus allowing the calorifiers to be purged and sediment / debris to be flushed.
records.Observation__c: The calorifier drain valve is sited at the hot water flow pump HWS(F)-01 with no drain on the individual calorifiers. As such, engineers are unable to drain down the calorifiers to remove sediment / debris at the base of the calorifiers.
records.Question__c: Are there any other concerns relating to hot water services?
records.Action2__c: Consult the operating / manufacturer’s instructions & top-up the combination boiler to a suitable operating water pressure. Combination boilers typically operate at a water pressure of 1.0 - 1.5 bar pressure.
records.Action3__c: Consideration should be given to the installation of a HWS flow pump(s) that is / are sufficiently rated to satisfactorily pump hot water around the hot water circuit, thus ensuring that hot water achieves a temperature of between 50oC and 60oC within 1 minute of flushing at all outlets. Alternatively, consideration should be given to providing hot water from low volume independent hot water heaters, supplied directly from mains cold water, with hot water services pipework supplied from the hot water calorifier cut back to its point of connection with existing services and capped off such that no deadleg remains.
records.Observation2__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, low hot water temperatures within the assessor to describe were found due to low water pressure to the assessor to name combination boiler with a water pressure of only xx bar pressure.
records.Observation3__c: At the time of the Risk Assessment, temperature profiles on the assessor to describe supplied from hot water calorifier CAL-01 achieved temperatures of less than 50oC after 1 minute of flushing. A temperature of greater than 50oC was established after xx minutes of flushing.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are there any other concerns relating to hot water services?

records.Name: RAQ-2610707
records.Action__c: Confirm that temperature profiles of stored and incoming water of the integral cold water storage reservoir/header tank(s) are undertaken on a 12 monthly basis with a maximum permitted cold water storage temperature of 25oC where it is serving other domestic outlets or 38oC when serving the hot water vessel only, along with visual inspections carried out, with the hygienic condition noted for levels of sediment, signs of stagnation, the formation of a bio-film, signs of venting of hot water or the deterioration on thermal insulation leading to favourable conditions for the survival & proliferation of legionella bacteria. All such actions must be recorded into an appropriate water services logbook Where inspection shows the integral cold water storage reservoir/header tank to be in a poor hygienic state, the cold water section should be physically cleaned to remove debris, sediment, corrosion or any other contaminants Where the cold stored water regularly exceeds the maximum permitted cold water storage temperature of 25oC  particularly during periods of low usage such as overnight or at weekends, consideration should be given to installing a timer clock, which switches off the immersion heater, The timer should be set to switch the immersion heater on again in time to ensure the water is heated sufficiently to achieve microbial control before use.
records.Observation__c: There are no records held on-site to indicate that integral cold water storage reservoir/header tank(s) are subject to visual inspection or temperature profiles of stored and incoming water are undertaken on a regular basis for signs of venting of hot water or deterioration on thermal insulation between the hot & cold sections, leading to favourable conditions for the survival & proliferation of legionella bacteria.
records.Question__c: Are the integral CWS Reservoirs associated with Fortic Cylinders or  Water Heaters with integral CWS Reservoirs subject to regular inspection.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Are the integral CWS Reservoirs associated with Fortic Cylinders or  Water Heaters with integral CWS Reservoirs subject to regular inspection.

records.Observation__c: There are no records held on site to indicate that temperature profiles are undertaken on a monthly basis.

All such actions must be recorded into an appropriate domestic water services logbook to illustrate due diligence.

records.Name: RAQ-2610712
records.Action__c: The outlets should be flushed for a minimum of 2 minutes on a weekly basis to prevent the stagnation of water within the supply pipework. Alternatively, the supply pipework should be valved off, cut back at the point of connection with existing services and capped off such that no deadlegs remain.
records.Observation__c: The following hot & cold water taps / outlets are deemed low usage and there are no formal records to indicate that they are subject to a flushing regime: assessor to list taps / outlets

records.Name: RAQ-2610713
records.Action__c: Ensure that temperature profiles of stored and incoming water from cold water storage are undertaken on a six monthly basis, along with visual inspections carried out, the hygienic condition noted for levels of sediment, signs of stagnation and the formation of a bio-film. Should visual inspection show that the cistern is deemed to be in a poor hygienic state, then a full physical clean & disinfection should be undertaken, with the cistern re-lined and any remedial works undertaken where applicable. Moderate to High levels of sediment found within the base of the cistern requires the cold water storage cistern to be subject to a full cleaning & disinfection in accordance with ACoP L8 and BS 8558:2011 (replaces BS6700:2006). All such actions must be recorded into an appropriate water services logbook.
records.Observation__c: There are no records held on-site to indicate that cold water storage cistern(s) CWS-01 & CWS-02 is / are subject to visual inspection or temperature profiles of stored and incoming water are undertaken on a six monthly basis.

records.Name: RAQ-2610716
records.Action__c: The assessment should be reviewed regularly and, whenever there is reason to suspect that it is no longer valid. An indication of when to review the assessment and what needs to be reviewed should be recorded. This may result from, for example: 1. changes to the water system or its use 2. changes to the use of the building in which the water system is installed 3. the availability of new information about risks or control measures 4. the results of checks indicating that control measures are no longer effective 5. changes to key personnel 6. a case of Legionnaires' disease/legionellosis is associated with the system
records.Observation__c: The ACoP L8 fourth edition states that the assessment should be reviewed regularly and, whenever there is reason to suspect that it is no longer valid.
records.Question__c: Have there been any changes to the use of the building since the last risk assessment?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Have there been any changes to the use of the building since the last risk assessment?

records.Name: RAQ-2610717
records.Action__c: Regular monitoring including where necessary water sampling is required to ensure that control measures are effective and to recognise out of control situations. In addition, a management plan is required to implement effective corrective action and record keeping of all control actions.
records.Observation__c: At the time of the risk assessment, records suggest that control measures are ineffective, with out of range temperature profiles / out of range analytical results etc
records.Question__c: Have there been any poor monitoring results that suggest that control measures are not working.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Have there been any poor monitoring results that suggest that control measures are not working.

records.Name: RAQ-2610718
records.Action__c: Ensure a regular review of the control scheme including legionella sampling from the affected area, Additionally, records should be kept up to date and available for inspection.
records.Observation__c: Information supplied to the Risk Assessor at the time of the Risk Assessment confirm there has been a case/s of legionnaires disease associated with this property.
records.Question__c: Have there been any cases of legionnaires disease associated with the system?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Have there been any cases of legionnaires disease associated with the system?

records.Name: RAQ-2610719
records.Action__c: Ensure that where possible, POU filters are only used as a temporary control measure. A review of the current control measures should be undertaken to determine the cause of a positive legionella sample. Once rectified, all POU filters should be removed and legionella sampling should be continued on a monthly basis, until there is no longer legionella bacteria present. • Records of the start date and lifespan / end date should be made available. Usually, a label with this information is fitted to the filter. These should be renewed and replaced on a frequency determined by the manufacturer. • In healthcare premises, long term usage of these filters may be permitted. Records of the installation / service of POU filters and lifespan / end date should be kept, with filters being renewed and replaced according to manufacturer’s guidelines
records.Observation__c: POU filters have been installed onto specify which outlets throughout the premises, indicating that positive legionella bacterium has been identified. POU filters prevent the discharge of planktonic legionella and other potentially pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria and parasites) from the outlets. POU filters should only be installed as a temporary measure whilst a permanent safe engineering solution is developed.
records.Question__c: Have POU filters been identified within the premises?
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Have POU filters been identified within the premises?

records.Name: RAQ-2610724
records.Action__c: A water services logbook or e-logbook system should be set up to include domestic water services and held on site. The water services logbook must include a management structure & contact details to show full lines of management communications within JD Sports Fashion PLC and the building representatives, should be produced. In addition, the following management system sections should be included into an appropriate water services logbook: -Log detailing visits by contractors -Faults Log -Correspondence -Out of date information

records.Name: RAQ-2610726
records.Action__c: Consideration should be given to training responsible management on a certified ‘Legionella Awareness Course’ and ensure it is adequately documented. Please Note: the Store Manager is required to undertake monthly temperature profiles and formally record this within the H&S/FS logbook. The Store Manager has not received any formal or informal training and is unaware of the potential risks; Hot Water: 50 to 60 degrees C (after 1 min of flushing) Cold Water: Less than 20 degrees C (after 2 min of flushing) Thermostatically Mixed Water: 37 to 42 degrees C  plus or minus 1 degree (at TMV or thermo tap outlet). Temperatures in excess of 60 degrees C can present risk of scalding and should be avoided.
records.Observation__c: It could not be verified if responsible persons for Health & Safety have received Water Safety / Legionella Basic Awareness training.

records.Name: RAQ-2610728
records.Action__c: It should be ensured that a written procedure is adhered to when undertaking maintenance work of heating F & E water systems, fire sprinkler system and fire hose reel system to prevent the creation of an aerosol and the dissemination of Legionella bacteria. Precautionary measures include minimising the number of persons present and by the use of respiratory protection where necessary.

records.Name: RAQ-2610729
records.Action__c: Ensure a planned preventative maintenance programme is implemented maintained and monitored to prevent the risk of Legionellosis. In addition all actions that need to be taken to reduce the bacterial growth potential or any other action is required to reduce the overall risk.

records.Name: RAQ-2610730
records.Action__c: The ACoP L8 recommends as “good practice” that water samples are collected for microbiological analysis on a 6 monthly basis. There is no legal requirement for water samples to be collected for analysis, however to illustrate “due diligence” it is prudent to undertake a regime of water quality monitoring, to ensure that the conditions within the water systems do not support and encourage proliferation of Legionella bacteria and other water-borne bacteria. Typical water monitoring regime includes: Mains Cold Water - Total Viable Counts (TVC's) at 22 & 37, Total Coliforms & E-coli Cold Water Services - Legionella, Total Viable Counts (TVC's) at 22 & 37, Total Coliforms & E-coli Hot Water Services – Legionella only
records.Observation__c: There are no records held on-site to indicate that water samples are collected on a six monthly basis.
records.Question__c: Has legionella sampling been carried out where control levels (temperature, biocide etc.) of the treatment regime have not been maintained at the required level?   Where water sampling has been carried out was it completed by a UKAS accredited laboratory?
records.Action2__c: Where monitoring for legionella is considered appropriate in hot and cold water systems, sampling should be carried out in accordance with BS 7592 Sampling for Legionella organisms in water and related materials. These should ideally be carried out on a 6 monthly basis. Analysis of water samples for legionella should be performed in UKAS-accredited laboratories with the current ISO standard methods for the detection and enumeration of legionella included within the scope of accreditation. These laboratories should also take part in a water microbiology proficiency testing scheme (such as that run by PHE or an equivalent scheme accredited to ISO 17043). Alternative quantitative testing methods may be used as long as they have been validated using ISO 17994 and meet the required sensitivity and specificity. Typical water monitoring regime includes: • Mains Cold Water - Total Viable Counts (TVC's) at 22 & 37, Total Coliforms & E-coli • Cold Water Services - Legionella, Total Viable Counts (TVC's) at 22 & 37, Total Coliforms & E-coli • Hot Water Services – Legionella only Subsequent records of sampling should be kept within a log book for a period of up to 5 years.
records.Observation2__c: Although microbiological samples are not usually required on a system supplied by mains cold water, there is currently an inadequate control scheme within the premises.  As temperature profiles are taken quarterly / bi-annually / annually, it is impossible to determine whether hot and cold water temperatures are in the required parameters stipulated in HSG 274 part 2. Legionella sampling should be considered when the target control parameters are not met ( i.e where temperatures are below 50 °C for hot water services  or exceed 20 °C for cold water systems) as outlined within HSG 274 part 2, paragraphs 2.120 to 2.124. Sampling should also be considered from areas subject to low usage, stagnation, excess storage capacity, dead legs / blind ends, excessive heat loss / heat gain, cross flow from the water system or any other anomaly.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c:
Has legionella sampling been carried out where control levels (temperature, biocide etc.) of the treatment regime have not been maintained at the required level?  

Where water sampling has been carried out was it completed by a UKAS accredited laboratory?

records.Name: RAQ-2610733
records.Action__c: It should be ensured that a written policy is produced to outline the necessary action to be taken for dealing with a Legionella outbreak. This may include the isolation of water services, cleaning and disinfecting or pasteurising the water system. The written procedure must include full lines of communication, including names of responsible persons, along with telephone / mobile numbers and e-mail addresses.
records.Observation__c: There is no documentation held on site regarding an action plan for dealing with a Legionella outbreak.
records.Question__c: Is there an action plan for dealing with a legionella positive sample results or legionella outbreak?
records.Action2__c: It should be ensured that a written policy is produced to outline the necessary action to be taken for dealing with the isolation of Legionella bacteria within monitoring samples. This may include the isolation of water services, cleaning and disinfecting or pasteurising the water system. The written procedure must include full lines of communication, including names of responsible persons, along with telephone / mobile numbers and e-mail addresses.
records.Observation2__c: There is no documentation held on site regarding an action plan for dealing with a Legionella positive sample result.
records.QuestionSet_Question__c: Is there an action plan for dealing with a legionella positive sample results or legionella outbreak?
